I'm able to execute the unit tests on Windows. On Ubuntu (16.04), however, an ImportError occurs:
```
python -m dtls.test.unit   
/usr/bin/python: cannot import name PROTOCOL_SSLv3
```
The ssl module does not define that name on that platform.

More generally, we should stick with importing the ssl module only if patch.py's do_patch is actually called, considering that the package's \_\_init\_\_ imports the patch module. PyDTLS should work in environments where the ssl module does not exist.

It's hard to say if it's a good idea or not.

In most cases, the DBus Introspection XML is a part of a pre-existing standard (like MPRIS), and the programmer can simply copy the XML file without needing to worry how to exactly replicate its behavior in his Python code. I guess we could also provide a object validator that automatically checks if all required methods are implemented, and that they take correct number of arguments; or even a XML to Python skeleton converter for quick starting implementation.

However, when there is no pre-existing standard, and the programmer is creating a new one, it can be quite easier to write everything in Python, and automatically generate XML data - as XML is not really a nice format to write by hand.

Also, having the argument data types specified in the Python source code might be useful for its readability. In fact, we could automatically test if the parameters have correct types even when methods are not called through DBus, but from other Python code - which might be useful for autotests, and for objects that are both published on DBus and used internally by the application. This suggests that type-related decorators could be implemented as a generic, non-dbus related tool, like signals in pydbus.generic.

But we don't always need type decorators. Without them, we can generate a skeleton XML file - listing all the methods and their argument names; but specyfing their arguments as "TODO" - to help programmer with writing the XML file.

I guess, we can split this feature into two smaller ones - the one about generic type-related decorators; and the one about generating DBus XML from a given Python class. And the first one should expose metadata that will let the second one automatically fill out the TODOs, and let the user publish the object without touching the XML at all. Both are useful and self-contained, and together they have synergy.

As an added bonus, developers will be able to quickly change from using autogenerated XML to using a XML file when they decide to freeze the API and publish it as a standard; without a need to remove type decorators, as they're still a useful part of the code.

Ideally there should be a cmd parameter because some poor people need to
use utf16, which can't be made sense of without very special treatment.

But more importantly, default behavior should be to work on raw, undecoded
bytes. As long as you never try to split cell contents (e.g. you must
output "[abc->aBc]" and not "a[b->B]c" which might split a character in the
middle in utf8), every other encoding I'm aware of would work just fine,
including utf8, DOS codepages, ISO codepages and Windows codepages (I must
admit I have no idea how pre-Unicode chinese/japanese codepages work, but
they would probably be fine too).

@vmarceau there is one small complication here... Uphill/downhill is calculated in `get_uphill_downhill()` and moving data is in `get_moving_data()`. Calculating speed could naturally belong to `get_moving_data()`, but... It's not enough to just check the elevation between two points to find out if the track segment is uphill/downhill, that's because of random elevation errors. You would end up with too many uphill/downhill segments even when you are on the same elevation all the time. To fix that, `get_uphill_downhill()` first "smooths" the elevation graph, but `get_moving_data()` don't. 

Maybe `get_uphill_downhill()` should do the same, or maybe `get_moving_data()` should be extended to calculate all the data `get_uphill_downhill()` *on a smoothed* track. That would also mean that `get_uphill_downhill()` should be deprecated. I'm still undecided on this.

That's an interesting point to discuss, I haven't really thought about that. Just for reference: the current state of affairs is that for removing tables we have `db.purge_table(name)` and `db.purge_tables()`, for deleting elements `table.delete(query)` and `table.purge()` as equivalents.

I think the question to ask here is what is more intuitive for the user. Maybe consistency between removing tables and removing elements is what users would expect. Personally I feel like removing tables and removing elements is different enough to justify an inconsistency here, but I've worked on TinyDB far too long to have an outsider's view on that. What do you think?

Another point we shouldn't ignore is that the current functions have been around for most of the time that TinyDB exists so we should be certain that it's worth having a breaking change and thus releasing TinyDB 4.0 for that change only.

I finally managed to have a good mapping for my case. The issue was masked by the fact that `main.py` doesn't handle well `ValueError` exceptions triggered during transaction parsing at deeper level, f.i., when the transaction has keys with empty value as in CVS exports with separate debit/credit columns. 

 Moreover, it looks like `sys.exit` is recommended with Python 3 -- or at least `ipdb` didn't like it... 

So a minimal patch might be:
```
--- main.py.orig	2018-02-20 21:18:02.751228108 +0100
+++ main.py.new	2018-02-22 18:47:14.111686097 +0100
@@ -24,7 +24,7 @@
 import itertools as it
 import traceback
 
-from sys import stdin, stdout
+from sys import stdin, stdout, exit
 from importlib import import_module

You have two options.
If using XML, to retrieve all option types:
```python
# To get all field types
pkt.bootp.option_type.all_fields

# To get all request items
pkt.bootp.option_request_list_item.all_fields
```
Note that those results will return a LayerField type object. You can access the value in various ways, so see which one is best for you. The available fields of LayerField for accessing the value are:
'base16_value',
 'binary_value',
 'get_default_value',
 'hex_value',
 'int_value',
 'raw_value',
 'showname',
 'showname_key',
 'showname_value'

If using JSON:
```python
# This will retrieve the type numbers.
pkt.bootp.type
# This will retrieve the type data, in the same order as the numbers above
pkt.bootp.type_tree
# For easier access you can do:
type_dict = dict(zip(pkt.boot.type, pkt.bootp.type_tree))
```
From the type dict or directly from the tree you can get the request_list_item.

The advantage of using JSON is that it's faster.

A more detailed explanation: The difference in the structure of XML and JSON packets is that XML fields are all "flat" on the original layer and you must use `.all_fields` if there are multiple ones. In JSON, as you can see, a type can return lists and fields may be inside other fields.

I believe the JSON method is better, but I did not want to change the XML one do it will remain backwards compatible.

Please close this issue if the explanation was satisfactory.

Dor.

As an update, I tried the ODE integrator from scipy and it pleases me to know that it gets a result that is quite consistent with the other approach. However, it is actually 2-3 times slower for a vectorized approach of the same size.

The Q I have is indeed an array of quaternions that gets constructed from the rotation angles  and the rotation axis at each step (since they change over time), similar to this simple example (to demonstrate how I construct my Q):


```
import numpy as np
import quaternion as quat

v = [3,5,0] #vector to be rotated
axis = [4,4,1]
theta = 1.2 

vector = np.array([0.] + v)
rot_axis = np.array([0.] + axis)
axis_angle = (theta*0.5) * rot_axis/np.linalg.norm(rot_axis) 

vec = quat.quaternion(*v)
qlog = quat.quaternion(*axis_angle)
q = np.exp(qlog)

v_prime = q * vec * np.conjugate(q)
```

Hi @Francks11!

While this is a nice idea, it won't work properly in all cases.

For example, in [this demo](http://jsfiddle.net/Mottie/856bzzeL/256/) there are multiple tables. Click on the "Test 1" button, then start pressing tab. Each header is focused (for accessibility; pressing <kbd>Enter</kbd> will sort the column), then the second "Test 2" button, then each header of the second table. The filters are completely skipped!.

If you start by clicking in the first filter then pressing tab, the filters in the first table are focused in sequence, the the filters in the second table. Then it starts at the "Test 1" button.

Another issue is setting the [`tabindex` property](http://www.w3.org/TR/html5/editing.html#negative-tabindex) to numbers greater than zero.

> If the value is greater than zero
The user agent must set the element's tabindex focus flag, should allow the element to be reached using sequential focus navigation, and should place the element in the sequential focus navigation order so that it is:
* before any focusable element whose tabindex attribute has been omitted or whose value, when parsed, returns an error,
* before any focusable element whose tabindex attribute has a value equal to or less than zero,
* after any element whose tabindex attribute has a value greater than zero but less than the value of the tabindex attribute on the element,
* after any element whose tabindex attribute has a value equal to the value of the tabindex attribute on the element but that is earlier in the document in tree order than the element,
* before any element whose tabindex attribute has a value equal to the value of the tabindex attribute on the element but that is later in the document in tree order than the element, and
* before any element whose tabindex attribute has a value greater than the value of the tabindex attribute on the element.

Which is different from setting it to zero (the `tabindex` that tablesorter adds to headers & filter elements)

> If the value is a zero
The user agent must set the element's tabindex focus flag, should allow the element to be reached using sequential focus navigation, and should follow platform conventions to determine the element's relative order.

The truncation error happens because when binding variables pyODBC sets the column size to number of characters (so for u'🌜🌜' that would be 2), but the driver expects the number of UTF-16 code units, and the moons are higher Unicode characters so each one is 2 code units, thus the column size should be set to 4.

It works in 4.0.22 because that version uses automatic detection of SQL types and column sizes using SQLDescribeParam to bind variables

I made a pull request with one way of solving this problem:
https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/pull/434

P.S.
The reason direct query like:
sql = "insert into test(user,  category)  values ('abcd', '🌜🌜🌜')"
inserts junk is because the Unicode string should have an N in the front, like:
sql = "insert into test(user,  category)  values ('abcd', N'🌜🌜🌜')"

More info here:
https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/t-sql/data-types/nchar-and-nvarchar-transact-sql?view=sql-server-2017

Relevant quote:
"Prefix Unicode character string constants with the letter N. Without the N prefix, the string is converted to the default code page of the database. This default code page may not recognize certain characters."

I don't think this is necessary anymore after the complete Unicode rewrite.  You can now use something like: `Connection.setdecoding(pyodbc.SQL_VARCHAR, encoding='utf-8', to=str)`

Thanks though.

I understand your interest in `callproc` as "a neat one-liner", but a generic implementation of `callproc` for any arbirtary ODBC driver might get complicated. For example, you ask

> I was wondering whether you could use the same approach used in pymssql.

pymssql tried to use the same approach as [the MySQL Connector/Python implementation of callproc](https://dev.mysql.com/doc/connector-python/en/connector-python-api-mysqlcursor-callproc.html) which was to have it return the sequence of arguments that were passed to it with the OUTPUT parameters replaced by their updated values. As it turned out, that didn't really work so well for pymssql because under the TDS protocol the output parameter values are sent *after* all of the result sets generated by the stored procedure itself. 

So for an MS_SQL stored procedure, in order to have the output parameter values available immediately after invoking `callproc`, pyodbc might have to retrieve and cache all of the result sets produced by the stored procedure. That would presumably prevent our Python application from retrieving result sets one-at-a-time and incrementally (via `fetchone()` or `fetchmany()`), and could greatly increase the memory requirements of that application if the result sets were large.

The issue is that by the time Sphinx has processed an RST file into a docutils tree, I think it's too late to bail out and remove it from the set. And it's definitely possible to hook into Sphinx and modify the list of files to process, but that happens well before the files are parsed. So there's something of a chicken/egg problem here: you want look at some data from the parse, but you want to look at it before Sphinx has parsed the files.

It's probably worth digging into Sphinx to see if there's a call we _can_ make to drop a file from the set after parse, or at least after it's read.

`check-manifest` runs `python setup.py sdist` twice: once in the (possibly-dirty) current working directory, and once in a clean VCS export.

I suspect you're running `python setup.py sdist --dry-run --verbose` in the possibly-dirty current working directory. This means `setup.py sdist` might pick up additional files for one of the following reasons
- you have a setuptools plugin that reads version control metadata and automatically adds those files to your sources
- you have an \*.egg-info directory left over from an earlier build that has a sources.txt, which makes setuptools pick up all those files as well.

When `check-manifest` performs a clean sdist in a tree that has all the VCS-versioned files without the actual VCS metadata (e.g. no `.git` directory), and no build artifacts (stale \*.egg-info), those additional sources of source files are eliminated.

The goal of check-manifest is to make sure MANIFEST.in is sufficient even in the absence of setuptools plugins/stale data.  I've felt the pain from debugging an issue where a package would include all but one \*.po files, because, turns out after I made the previous release I've upgraded Subversion (and setuptools could no longer understand the format of .svn/entries), and so the old \*.po files were picked up because they were present in the stale egg-info, but newly-added \*.po files would not be picked up. Have you tried `q` instead of `<C-c>`?

More scrolls are controlled by `pagesize`. I don't think there's anything special about `<C-d>`; any key after `<C-c>` would do, which is why `` ``` gets eaten. The console will scroll more by `min(pagesize, console height)` as best I can tell. However, after `<C-c>` it scrolls a line at a time for some reason. If there is more input, the lines are the next input line, `--Break--`, `r(1);`, and the next line. If there is no more input, the lines are just the last 3.

At least that is how it is on my system. If pagesize is 4 or higher, as best I can tell, `q` will correctly break more. However, by that time I suppose `--more--` will have already eaten the backtick for the hash...

The legacy namespace is really there as a stopgap - to provide some illusion of backwards compatibility.

There are building blocks in luma.Core now that allow you to move a viewport over a larger virtual device. This can be programmed to give the appearance of scrolling. There have been questions to make scroll message scroll in any direction, work with asyncio, and yours about interrupting it if there is a higher priority message.

By providing the mechanism to scroll, you can implement it to suit your individual needs, rather than the library providing a kitchen-sink version that is complicated and does everything.

So think of the legacy version as a simple reference version, and no, there most likely won't be any more scroll implementations in the main codebase. It is likely that there maybe demos and examples added instead to show you what is possible though.

I think `file-templates` as an application procedure is slightly unclear. How I see it `templates` and `file-templates` are definition keys with different scopes. `defaults` and `file-defaults` could be appliers with different scopes. This allows for locally scoped templates as well as their applications. Continuing with your examples:

~/.ssh/assh.d/sam.yml

```
# define file specific templates
file-templates:
  shared-template:
    User: file-specific-user

# apply it together with other templates
file-defaults:
  Inherits:
  - sam-hosts
  - idrsa-hosts
  - shared-template

hosts:
  ghi-server:  # inherits from contromaster-hosts, sam-hosts, idrsa-hosts _&_ shared-template
    Inherits:
    -  controlmaster-hosts
    HostName: 2.3.4.5
  jkl-server:  # inherits from sam-hosts, idrsa-hosts _&_ shared-template
    HostName: 6.7.8.9
```

The only important think to consider is that the rules of precedence should be very clear. For instance if two templates define `User` then it should be clear how `assh` will choose the one to use.

What do you think?

`[[(308, 65) (483, 299)]]` is not the same thing as `[(308, 65, 483, 299)]` at all.

If you pass in `[(308, 65, 483, 299)]` (i.e. 4 int elements in python tuple in a python list) as the array of known face locations, the existing code will take it and convert into to a dlib object automatically. You just need to pass in the data as the correct type. You don't need to do any extra work with dlib at all.

Here's a simple working example:

```
import face_recognition

image = face_recognition.load_image_file("biden.jpg")

face_locations = [(241, 740, 562, 419)]

face_landmarks_list = face_recognition.face_landmarks(image, face_locations)

print(face_landmarks_list[0]['chin'])
```

It's that simple. Just replace `face_locations = [(241, 740, 562, 419)]` with a call to your new face detector function (i.e `face_locations = myFaceDetector()`) that returns data *in the same format as that* with no numpy objects or anything like that.

*Summary:*
It seems R/SAS contain tools in their CoxPH packages to estimate a variance
of an individual's survival curve; provided a CoxPH model has been fit for
the sample population. I'll investigate a little further, but here is a
reference alluding to this point:

Therneau, Grambsch "Modeling Survival Data, extending the Cox model".
Chapter 10.2: Individual survival, Cox model. Figure 10.1 provides a plot
of upper/lower estimates which is especially attractive.

*Synthesis:*
Individual confidence intervals may be computed using the delta method.
Supposedly, such a method can be enriched to account for both tied events
and strata. As the authors note though, confidence intervals for
individuals may be hard to interpret. I wonder if robust estimates from
+cluster(id) may be applicable.

*Conclusion:*
This problem is a bit richer than I may have initially expected. For
example, the delta method argument due to Tsiatis in this setting was
derived "directly" from martingale theory by Andersen et al. Finally, it
may be interesting to start with Fleming-Harrington when using Efron as
ties to derive an individual's CI in Lifelines.

client.suite()  # returns empty suite object if you want to make a new suite

Also methods like add, delete, update just take an object and then will figure out what respective api method to call based on object type (suite, project, test....)

That isn't broken libs. The pip command must have also upgrade your other libraries to the newest version, including flask to version 0.11. In that version, they changed how they want you to import modules. No longer do they recommend doing
```python
from flask.ext.sqlalchemy import <foo>
```

The new way of doing it is
```python
from flask_sqlalchemy import <foo>
```

And the same for all the other extensions there. Those are just warnings saying that you should switch to using the new way on this newest version of flask. You can always move back to the older version of flask as well, if this gives you any problems.

As for the type error when using a valid token, I'll need to check it out and see what's happening there. If you have a stack trace handy that could help. Otherwise I'll rebuild the flask-restless demo app again and see if I can duplicate it.

Yes, that seems like a good idea. This is trivial to implement but will break the way `MapQuery` works. Right now you would call it like 

`result = api.Get(MapQuery(bbox))` 

Because the `/map` call is a different endpoint is actually a different endpoint, it may be confusing to define the wrapper like this. I propose either a static method:

`map = overpass.Map(bbox)`

or a new instance method:

`map = api.Map(bbox)`

Looking at how we have been designing the wrapper so far, I gravitate towards the latter solution.

We are using Box to store artwork/design images uploaded by our company's users via the Box Content Uploader UI Element. Currently, these images are all being uploaded into the same folder for all our users, so after they are successfully uploaded, we use the Box SDK to rename the files by appending the file id to the name, to prevent naming conflicts with any future files uploaded. We then store the ids of those files in our database for later use in our applications. In this case, we are only concerned with renaming the basename of the file, as the extension of the image could be anything, like .tif, .png, .jpg, and so on.

Later on in our workflow, we have bots regularly copy some of those image files to new, dynamically generated folder locations, and completely change the names of the files based on which record in our database the file is associated with. In that scenario, we are only referencing the file by id, and do not care about its current name or extension. We only want to copy the file over to the new location, rename the basename, and preserve the current extension.

Those are all very good points. Fido is an HTTP client, that's why I thought of following conventions of other widely used libraries (requests, concurrent.futures, python socket stdlib) which also are used in high-scale SOA (we ourselves use requests as a sync alternative to fido). 

I think what jolynch is suggesting makes a lot of sense when looking at this problem from the point of view of a highly critical SOA such as Yelp's. Nevertheless, sjaensch makes a good point in that we'd end up with different defaults in bravado depending on which underlying client was selected (Fido=finite timeout, requests=infinite).

I'd lean towards setting a default timeout in bravado itself, regardless of the underlying HTTP client (requests, fido... something else in the future?) and leave Fido following the conventions of other HTTP clients situated at a similar stack level.

Filters need to be supported at the service API level. Filters for things like `describe_tags` are just boto3 representations of an API parameter (e.g., http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AutoScaling/latest/APIReference/API_DescribeTags.html). I believe a rule of thumb is that any time you see a Filter parameter, you can assume that it does the filtering server side. Adding a filter parameter that does client side filtering would be inconsistent with the rest of the library, making boto3 code utilizing Filter parameters harder to understand with regard to the amount of data being sent over HTTP-- e.g., am I sending and retrieving more data than necessary because I'm filtering client side, or is this filter applied server side?

> Like a boto3 helper module that can do slightly higher level calls that do aws api calls behind the scenes with sorting and filtering and inverse filtering?

High level filtering and result object manipulation could be achieved client side after a response is received using [JMESPath](http://jmespath.org/). JMESPath supports filtering data, sorting, and various other methods for slicing up your data. For example, you could get the result of `describe_launch_configurations` and then extract only launch configurations that use a specific `ImageId`. Something like this should work:

```php
import jmespath

response = client.describe_launch_configurations()
jmespath.search(response, 'LaunchConfigurations[?ImageId == `"xyz"`]')
```

My OS is CentOS release 6.5, and I met the same problem.
The reason of the problem maybe that **libgit2.so.0.24.0** was put into the wrong directory(/usr/local/lib)
But **ldconfig**default to detect /lilb and /usr/lib
if you execute `sudo ldconfig -v | grep git` ,and don't see libgit2.so.0.24.0,you can fix as below

so we need copy /usr/local/lib/libgit2.so.0.24.0 to /usr/lib64, and rebuild the soft link
`#cp  /usr/local/lib/libgit2.so.0.24.0  /usr/lib64`
`cd /usr/lib64`
`sudo ln -s libgit2.so.0.24.0 libgit2.so.24`
`sudo ln -s libgit2.so.24 libgit2.so`

reexecute `sudo ldconfig -v | grep git` 
       libgit2.so.24 (libc6,x86-64) => /usr/lib64/libgit2.so.24
        libgit2.so (libc6,x86-64) => /usr/lib64/libgit2.so
Then you can use python -c "import pygit2"

`pypy --version` returns `Python 2.7.13` for both v5.8.0 and v5.9.0. It outputs the PyPy version number below that, but I don't think pipenv looks at that. As long as `platform_python_implementation` is `PyPy` and the `python_version` or `python_full_version` markers match, I believe pipenv just picks the first match it finds in the pyenv installed versions.

pipenv does not look at the shims to determine which python to use, it scans `$PYENV_ROOT/versions` for what is installed, adds them to the `$PATH` and finds the first one that matches the environment markers.
I believe https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/commit/d5b9b36713826746d69cde6d9256d33a528881f0 should fix this right up :)

> On latest RHEL system, 'rpm' is replaced by default with 'dnf'

I don't think this is quite correct. `rpm` is the low-level mechanism which manages the installation of packages in RPM format. Previous Fedora / Red Hat based distros (e.g. Fedora, RHEL, CentOS) used **`yum`** as their package manager which handles retrieving RPMs from remote repositories and installing them (using `rpm`). Newer distros have replaced `yum` with `dnf`. AFAIK, `rpm` is *still* used under the hood to manage installing RPM packages.

+++ /var/tmp/alt__init__.py	2017-08-11 15:05:16.319781794 +0200
@@ -2286,7 +2286,10 @@
         self.name = name
         self.module_name = module_name
         self.attrs = tuple(attrs)
-        self.extras = Requirement.parse(("x[%s]" % ','.join(extras))).extras
+        if extras:
+            self.extras = Requirement.parse(("x[%s]" % ','.join(extras))).extras
+        else:
+            self.extras = ()
         self.dist = dist
 
     def __str__(self):

> I'm not saying that we should not include Makefile, but the target audience of Makefile is different from the one that would like to see README.rst.

Consider a `Makefile` looking like:

```
# This is a minimal example application for Invenio-Records-UI.
#
# Prerequisites: Redis, SQlite.
#
# The example application demonstrates foo and bar. You can run xyzzy and zyxxy
# and see the output on http://0.0.0.0/records/1.
#
# ...

all:
   ...
```

Would having an extra README file be advantageous in this case? Isn't the verbiage at the start of Makefile enough?

That said, I'm not fully against including a minimal README either :smile: but I think it is not really necessary, because Makefile is a "runnable README" already. ("Less is more.") 

I assume that people have a habit of reading a Makefile when they meet one... we are targetting developers, they should be already familiar with the concept.

> Finally, what you can do is a file README.sh with a comment at the begining saying "run me to launch the example"

This approach would have the clutter disadvantage I mentioned above if you keep the tiny shell scripts for people to rerun the "populate" step or the  "destroy" step more than once.

Unless you thought of using README.sh together with Makefile? In that case, we don't need README.sh, it would be more than enough to use a plain-text README or a formatted README.rst. These are more standard, and possibly easier to include "as is" into RTFD documentation.

> If you only find a Makefile, you'll have to open it, and you'll need the technical skills to understand it.

Well, if a developer doesn't understand Makefiles yet, then it'll be a good investment for him/her to learn the concept :smile: Let's aim at using the right tool for the job.  Frankly, the concept shouldn't be hard to understand:

```
# This is some help text.

all: build run init populate test

init:
    flask db init
    flask db create

populate:
    flask fixtures records

destroy:
    flask db drop
``` 

> so it is well printed in Github

I reckon this was not my primary goal. I think that random visitors interested in an Invenio package would tend to browse our RTFD pages in order to learn more about the package, and Invenio developers would have the code checked out locally,  so "browsing code directories on GitHub" wasn't of top concern to me.

Instead, I've been calling for taking advantage of this RFC to aim for more than that, notably (i) to propagate example instructions into RTFD, (ii) to declutter the source code base of the current tiny shell scripts, (iii) to enable easy rerunning of example app steps for smoother development and integration testing, and all this (iv) without having to repeat the either the information or the instruction steps in several places, if at all possible.  

(We started these musings with @hachreak in https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-deposit/pull/122 and this RFC seemed like a good place to take over and conclude on these thoughts...)

Well, I think it is clear that I disagree to @hannosch at that point, as I have done this Pull Request.

I think we should devide two topics:

* isort --> Tool and Sorting of Imports
* flake8 --> Checking and enforcing Coding Conventions

As we did start this discussion face to face, this should be also a kind of documentation.

Coding conventions are something we both welcome. Code, especially Python Code should be easyly readable to help understand it. As Zope Packages are older than PEP8 and PEP20 most of the naming conventions did not match, but as PEP8 states that if Project specific Code Conventions exists those have precedence.

This Pull Request is about enforcing Code Conventions. Therefore the tox and travis-ci configuration have corresponding sections for flake8 and isort (actually 2, isort --> isort in check only mode, isort-apply --> allways commented out in active parts, it could be used to apply the sort and grouping settings)

To the Code Convention for me belongs also rules about imports. 
We did start by applying Plone Code Conventions (https://docs.plone.org/develop/styleguide/python.html) as those are the only documented which in Zope/Plone Context exists today.

One specific part of it is Grouping and sorting Imports (https://docs.plone.org/develop/styleguide/python.html#about-imports)
For me it doesn't matter which rules for grouping and sorting are applied, but it should be a defined rule. 
For RestrictedPython @icemac and me agreed on applying the Plone standard rules.

The isort rules (the are just checked by the tests, not automatically applied) are defined in setup.cfg [isort], documentation about how to configure could be found at http://isort.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#configuring-isort 
 
The other point @hannosch had, is that the import order in Zope Context could sometimes be very problamatic as it produced circular dependencies. Also Moved Implementations and backwards compatibility imports might be warned about. Both is no problem in this context, as enforcing the code conventions allows you to mark specific test(e.g. # NOQA: E401  # BBB import) so that no warning is shown. This also enforces developers to think and comment why this is in a specific order or imported at all. 

In summary I am ok to discuss which isort rules should be applied, but I strongly request to enforce code conventions.

A small addition: I can think of other reasons why the `future` package might be downloaded on Python 3.x, but to confirm my suspicions I'll need to take a look at the actual Python packages involved. For example wheel distributions support conditional dependencies, e.g. to mark a dependency as Python 2.x only. Source distributions require other handling to implement this same logic, so if pip prefers a wheel distribution but pip-accel for some reason prefers the source distribution, you could be seeing different (though possibly harmless) behavior.

> How would I do this? In a text based format with a git backend I would 
> select "edit entry", commit the change directly or via a pull request, 
> and the responsible person could merge the change.
> How would this work in the case of HumanCyc?
>
We have a GUI editing interface within Pathway Tools.  There is a 
different forms-based
editing tool for every datatype we support, e.g., for proteins, 
reactions, metabolites, pathways, etc.
For each one of those datatypes, there is a separate interactive dialog 
that shows the user
the current values of various fields in the database, allows the user to 
changes those fields,
and then saves the edits into the database.  These are the tools our 
biologist curators use
to update the database.
>
> How are you tracking changes to the entries in the database? Is there 
> a complete log of changes by whom (for attribution)? Both the changing 
> of information and attribution are "for free" with a git based system.
>
We have a complete log of all update transactions, stored within the 
database.  It can
be queried via SQL.  Yes, it includes a record of who made the update.
>
>     Just as to update a shared git repository, a user has to be
>     granted access to the repository. So one could just as well accuse
>     a git-based project of "hiding information in a private repository".
>
> This is not true. Everybody can make a pull request, no login, user 
> data, account or anything needed. You can promote users to trusted 
> commiters, but everybody can propose changes in a simple manner.
>
I thought there were private projects in git?  Maybe not.  So OK, 
changes can be proposed in a simple
manner (if you call it simple for someone to understand the XML well 
enough to make a change),
but still to commit directly the person editing must be authorized.
>
>     The XML-based formats used by many model developers are not easy
>     to update and are not at all git-friendly.
>
> I completely agree. They are good as exchange formats, but not suited 
> to track changes.
>
And there is another series limitation of exchange formats, I think 
raised earlier in this thread,
which is that if the file is written by a software tool (e.g., an SBML 
editing tool), there is no guarantee
that the tool will write the file with objects (e.g., reactions) in the 
same order or with line breaks or
other whitespace in the same place.  These syntactic changes to the file 
could cause thousands of
conflicts and render git-based change tracking useless.  So all tools 
used for editing must be
controlled carefully to be sure they do not misbehave in this sense.
>
>     I really hope the community is working into this direction instead
>     of hiding information in in-house databases.
>
> Yes, this happens independent of the the used technology, but is 
> dependent on the license of the data. There are open databases like 
> Reactome (CCO) and there are private repositories and vica versa.
> Unfortunately, this is the impression I get when looking at the 
> subscription model behind HumanCyc. Why should I curate an entry in 
> HumanCyc if these is subsequently licensed for someone else and only 
> accessible after others pay?
>
OK, now you are getting into non-technical issues, which is fine. I've 
tried for 20 years to
convince government funding agencies to fund curation of the many 
organisms they have
paid to sequence, but they refuse to do it, they only support curation 
of a small number of
organisms.  BioCyc is now exploring use of a different model, the 
subscription model, to
raise funds for curation.  We do not make a profit from BioCyc 
subscription revenues, they
are all re-invested into BioCyc itself to create a stronger resource for 
the scientific community.
Just as libraries buy subscriptions from journals and scientists write 
journal articles that are
only available to subscribers, we are using a similar approach to fund 
BioCyc.

Best wishes,

Peter

After some deliberation, I think I categorize this as a feature request as opposed to a bug.  I think `dill` has the right (default) behavior currently, but it should be possible to ship a module without requiring it to be in the `PYTHONPATH` of the target machine.  If there are unpicklable items therein, then so be it… it will fail.  It could be handled quite simply in porting the source of the module.  It opens up a huge can of worms… and will likely fail if there are dependencies that are also not shipped.  However, how different is this than the options that exist in `dill` for shipping a *file* associated with a python `file` object?

So the problem I found on my previous code was that `pyfastcopy` uses a C python package called `pysendfile`, which is not cross-platform. The C code compiled on Linux in the builds couldn't be run on other platforms. Although I tested on Mac OS X before, the program I tested was originally built on Mac OS X, so I didn't realize the error until today. This `pyfastcopy` also caused the error that Radina reported above on Windows, as it was trying to import `mock`, but it didn't exist on Windows. 

What I've changed in code is to install `pyfastcopy` into `kolibri/dist/cext` folder and add its path to `sys.path` manually, as it is a C extension just like `cryptography`. However, since `pyfastcopy` is not working on Mac OS X and Windows, I've changed the code so that `pyfastcopy` will only be installed and used on Linux platforms. 

I've tested the pex file on Mac OS X, Ubuntu 16.04 and windows installer on Windows 10, doing import from network, import from/export to USB. The export to USB is working once I manually change the `block_size` to 2097152 as stated in issue #2425. Except that, everything looks good to me.

I've been able to get around this issue by writing my own 'footprint' function over the one in suds at server startup.  It's stood up to my testing, but obviously isn't anywhere near ideal; use at your own risk!

```python
EMPTY_LIST_PROPERTY_STRINGS = frozenset((
                                         'urls',
                                         'appUrls',
                                         'finalUrls',
                                         'finalMobileUrls',
                                         'finalAppUrls',
                                         ))

def adwords_empty_final_url_footprint(sobject):
    """
        Get the I{virtual footprint} of the object.
        This is really a count of the attributes in the branch with a significant value.
        @param sobject: A suds object.
        @type sobject: L{Object}
        @return: The branch footprint.
        @rtype: int
    """
    n = 0
    for a in sobject.__keylist__:
        v = getattr(sobject, a)
        if v is None: continue
        if isinstance(v, Object):
            n += adwords_empty_final_url_footprint(v)
            continue
        if hasattr(v, '__len__'):
            if len(v): n += 1
            # START HACKED IN CODE
            elif a in EMPTY_LIST_PROPERTY_STRINGS:
                n += 1
            # END HACKED IN CODE
            continue
        n += 1
    return n

# BE CAREFUL, the way you'll want to do the following step will depend on your application
# requirements; this is the absolute simplest case and assumes single-thread access of suds
# dedicated to use with adwords.
# Substitute the hacked function into the suds library
sudsobject.footprint = adwords_empty_final_url_footprint
```

Hello,

The reason for this is that the provided `GoogleOAuth2Client` implementations are expected to be initialized with a refresh token that is used to receive a valid access token. It will also refresh the access token as it gets close to expiry. The access token is short-lived, and the expectation is that most users will be using the library for an extended period, so they would benefit from an automatic refresh mechanism.

That said, if you would prefer to handle OAuth 2.0 differently, you are free to create a subclass of `GoogleOAuth2Client` that better meets your specifications.

Regards,
Mark

Here is a snippet to give you an idea. `$` calls a shell command once for the whole workflow, commands (e.g. in `init, cleanup, compile`) are references to Python modules, `$$` for each style while replacing `%f` with source path (defined in from).   name: Nassim

    # the &styles anchor is used here, so the list of styles can be referenced later
    styles: &styles [Regular, Italic, Medium, MediumItalic, Semibold, SemiboldItalic, Bold, BoldItalic, Extrabold, ExtraboldItalic]
    formats: [ufo, ttf]

  to:
    folder: ../../fonts/Nassim/
    family: Nassim
    # reference to styles specified above
    styles: *styles
    formats: [otf, ttf]
  cleanup:
    - mrhandy.fdk.cleanup
  init:
    - $ makeInstancesUFO -d Nassim-Uprights.designspace -a -c -n
    - $ makeInstancesUFO -d Nassim-Italics.designspace -a -c -n
    - $$ checkOutlinesUFO -e -all %f:
        formats: [ufo]
    - $$ autohint -q %f:
        formats: [ufo]
    - mrhandy.fdk.fkern:
        formats: [ufo]
    - mrhandy.fdk.mark:
        formats: [ufo]
        useExtension: true
  compile:
    - mrhandy.fdk.makeotf
    - mrhandy.post.ttfautohint:

    - mrhandy.post.addDSIG
```

_I'm going to paste in here a slightly revised snippet of a comment I made in a great conversation with @kalbasit. I think it's important to bubble this up._

_Background: My current prototype uses a chain-of-command pattern with dependency injection to create a composable architecture. Or, in plain English, it maps a named task ("boot") to a series of individual (reusable) steps. What I'm pasting below explains my rationale._

[L]et me hit on the big design goals of this rewrite:
1. Replace a bunch of shell with Go
2. Make everything really, really testable
3. Hopefully work toward a component-based system that we can re-use

The first is easy to accomplish.

For the other two, here's what I am thinking: Basically, builder's boot process runs a sequence of individual steps. Some of these steps are really specific, but most are fairly generic. Read a bunch of environment variables. Send a request to etcd. Start a system service.

So what I'm doing is taking a system that provides the ability to run a sequence of commands, and writing a bunch of re-usable commands. Since every command follows the same API contract, the system is very composable. It's designed for dependency injection, which makes writing good tests very easy as well -- whether unit tests or functional tests. And at the top level, it should be really easy for a new developer to look at the route description and figure out exactly what the process is.

Defining a route (task list, chain) looks like this:
```go
	reg.Route("boot", "Boot the builder.").
		// Get env vars
		Does(env.Get, "vars").
		Using("HOST").WithDefault("127.0.0.1").
		Using("ETCD_PORT").WithDefault("4001").
		Using("ETCD").WithDefault("http://$HOST:$ETCD_PORT").
		Using("ETCD_PATH").WithDefault("/deis/builder").
		Using("ETCD_TTL").WithDefault("20").
		// Are there cases where we need to support more than one URL?
		Does(etcd.Client, "client").
		Using("url").WithDefault("http://localhost:4001").From("cxt:ETCD").
		// See if etcd is awake. This is the legacy way of doing it.
		Does(etcd.Ls, "ls").
		Using("client").From("cxt:client").
		Using("retries").WithDefault(20).
		// Sleep while etcd clears its stale values. Do we really need to do this?
		Does(Sleep, "_").Using("duration").WithDefault(21*time.Second).
		// Generate the name of the users directory.
		Does(fmt.Sprintf, "newdir").
		Using("format").WithDefault("%s/users").
		Using("0").From("cxt:ETCD_PATH").
		// Create the users directory in etcd.
		Does(etcd.MakeDir, "mkdir").
		Using("path").From("cxt:newdir").
		Using("client").From("cxt:client")
```

Route `boot` does the following:
- Gets env vars and puts them in the context.
- Creates an etcd client 
- Runs an etcd `ls` command
- Sleeps for 21 seconds
- Creates a new directory name
- Makes a new directory

(This is the sequence that the current builder shell script runs)

Provided that every command is properly documented, it should be easy to then dive into the specifics of the code.

There are three things I am not particularly thrilled with:

1. `cookoo.Params` objects require a little extra documenting (or code-reading).
2. There are a lot of type assertions required in this approach.
3. `go fmt` is really strict on indentation, so more decorative whitespacing is not allowed, e.g.:
```
reg.Route(foo).
    Does(bar).
        Using(baz).WithDefault("OH HAI")
```

But these feel like minor annoyances when compared with the benefit of building a composed system like this.

_The library I'm using in the example above is `github.com/Masterminds/cookoo`._

Thanks, Michael.  Just in case it wasn't obvious what I was talking about in my original message, here's a concrete example of the way auto-commit could be used in code:
```python
# create a connection with auto-commit switched on (i.e. not the default of False)
conn = turbodbc.connect(dsn='...', autocommit=True)
crsr = conn.cursor()
# the following sql statement would not need to be explicitly committed
crsr.execute("INSERT INTO TABLE ...")
# it should still be possible to check auto-commit on the connection when necessary
if not conn.autocommit:
    conn.commit()
# dynamically change the auto-commit setting
conn.autocommit = False
# the following sql statement would now need to be explicitly committed
crsr.execute("INSERT INTO TABLE ...")
conn.commit()
```
Personally, I do use code like this with pyodbc.  I also set `autocommit=True` almost every time I create a connection.

When the value is a type, then PyDelphin constructs a TdlDefinition object for it, since it's possible for the type to define an AVM or be a compound type (in other words, PyDelphin does not have a separate conception of simple types). Therefore, I think you want to get at `l.supertypes`. The following works for me:

```python
from delphin import tdl

lex = '/home/goodmami/grammars/erg-1214/lexicon.tdl'

with open('lex2wn.log', 'w') as log, open(lex, 'r') as f:
    for le in tdl.parse(f):
        lid=le.identifier
        if 'SYNSEM' not in le:
            print ('No SYNSEM for {}'.format(le.identifier), file=log)
            continue
        elif 'SYNSEM.LKEYS.KEYREL.PRED' in le:
            pred = le['SYNSEM.LKEYS.KEYREL.PRED']
            if isinstance(pred, str):
                pass  # nothing to do
                print ('LKEYS.KEYREL.PRED is string for {}'.format(lid), file=log)
            elif isinstance(pred, tdl.TdlDefinition):
                assert len(pred.supertypes) == 1
                pred = pred.supertypes[0]
                print ('LKEYS.KEYREL.PRED is type for {}'.format(lid), file=log)
        else:
            print ('No LKEYS.KEYREL.PRED for {}'.format(le.identifier), file=log)
```

Btw, the API documentation is pretty sparse for the TDL module, but the unit tests are written as "literate" code, and are pretty informative: https://github.com/delph-in/pydelphin/blob/develop/tests/tdl_test.md. I should migrate this to proper documentation or at least make it more discoverable.

Hi Carson,

Thanks for reporting this issue.

Running these tests all at the same time might result in 429s as you see but I'm surprised that adding the delay of 15 secs between the tests is still causing the throttle. In the second case, are the same set of test cases failing? Probably, each of those tests are making several requests too soon causing the throttle within the same test case. Most of the collections in the tests should be S1 which support 250 RUs/second and I'd guess that would be sufficient for most of the test cases.

Performance tier is tied to a collection, so as long as you are creating new test collections there shouldn't be any impact of the other load(if any) on the same database.

We probably would need to add retry policy around 429s for these test cases, so that always succeed(even if they take time).

I'll add this to our backlog but feel free to contribute to it if you wish.

Regards,
Rajesh

I was able to repro with 4.1 and 4.2. Steps:

1. Connect from your browser to a remote notebook instance, or to an instance that you can temporarily halt network connectivity to.
2. Create a notebook.
3. Remove network connectivity so that your browser cannot talk to the kernel.
4. Try to execute a cell without network connectivity.
5. Wait for the `Connection failed` message to appear in the UI. By then, the console should show WebSocket connection to kernel failed and that it's retrying.
6. Reestablish network connectivity.
7. Console should say that kernel_connected.
8. Click OK in `Connection failed` dialogue.
9. Observe that disconnected icon is still in the status bar and cell is still supposedly executing.
10. New cell cannot be executed (maybe the frontend thinks the first cell hasn't finished execution and there's no way for the frontend to reconcile the state?)
11. Refresh your browser as the only way to get out of this state.

This is not related to terminado, which is what's used when you click 'New > Terminal' from the dashboard.

We have to specify *some* size for subprocesses running in a pseudoterminal, and 80x25 is a common default. It's also better to default to too small, which tends to make cramped output, than too big, which can completely break things like tables.

We could do something hackish to calculate a width from the JS and send it to the kernel every time it changes, but the existing output would still be wrong as soon as the notebook was displayed at a different width, so it doesn't seem worth the added complexity.

In kernels that can do so, a better approach is to produce an HTML table rather than ASCII art, which can be displayed neatly in the frontend. There isn't currently a way to display HTML from bash_kernel, though.

But the design of JupyterHub is focused on making it easy to have custom Authenticators (handle authentication) and Spawners (spawning servers for users). >> Flags that may be used by multiple layers need to be automatically cleared by charms.reactive, or multiple layers may take responsibility creating races.

> Could we just promote a convention that the charm layer is responsible for clearing the global flag, and any base layer should use triggers? Or, if the charm layer doesn't clear them, we could still just leave the flags set and have the framework do a clear + set to ensure that triggers and handlers are fired.

The charm layer cannot be responsible for clearing global flags set by base layers or charms.reactive, because it does not know what they all are.  If charms.reactive sets new flag like `charm.upgraded` and does not clear it, then every charms.reactive charm needs to be updated to clear that flag, even if they are not using it, because one of their base layers may be relying on it. Its a similar issue with the endpoint flags; if an interface layer documents that the endpoint.foo.changed flags are part of the API, then the interface layer becomes responsible for clearing those flags, because there may be several other layers relying on the flag and no way to coordinate which of these several other layers should be responsible for clearing the flag.

> I would suggest to add an option: --ttl-hack (disabled by default)

is for. Maybe it might be useful for connecting sshuttle to localhost,
but why would you want to do that?

to remove it???

On the otherhand, without this hack, and if a novice user does sshuttle
to localhost, the results could be... interesting. I guess it does offer
some protection here.

Agreed, It puzzles me why Microsoft has chosen to have multiple frameworks other than the fact that the full framework is years in the making and obviously still in heavy use. But, Why in the world would you not have a roadmap to make a better framework that can eventually replace both the full windows only versions and mono. There are plenty of API's that can be wrapped to provide Native UI and OS libraries that can be accessed in both Mac and Linux in addition to what works on windows. Just look at AvaloniaUI, ETO.Forms and Xamarin.Forms all prove that a consistent method can be provided to provide desktop application capability to .NETCore that will provide native interfaces across major OS's including mobile devices. 

Why not capitalize on this concept and once and for all provide a cross platform solution that allows developers to get things done. Why can't we write apps on any OS that can work anywhere including IOT and mobile devices with one unified framework. Also it IMHO is a crime that Microsoft has not embraced the entire .NET ecosystem which would in my opinion provide them with a clear winner for example, there have been many of languages ported or created to run on the platform, Boo, Java, Python, Ruby, PHP, Cobra, Cobol, Typescript, Javascript and several others. It baffles me why Microsoft does not support these efforts so that .Net can attract more usage I suspect many developers of PHP or Java or Ruby would love to be able to maintain their skill set using the dotnet core as it provides many benefits and allows for seamless integration not to mention it opens up a whole lot of mature code that has been written over the years to the dotnet ecosystem. It actually makes me upset to see addins and plugins for these languages fall unmaintained and become not usable in monodevelop, sharpdevelop and even VS has seen some come and go. It would be awesome to be able to have multiple langage projects using one framework to maximize the best of the entire development ecosystem not just one companies vision.

Now that .Net is opensource it would be wonderful to see these efforts united to bring the framework inline with what it should be and see these languages being ported to Roslyn and brought inline with the current modern direction and standards of the .net core as a whole. It's totally possible and as example php is currently being ported to the Roslyn stack and dotnet core it's called peachpie and is the natural evolution of the Phalanger project. How come nobody tries to embrace a community instead of just their vision? Why can't we use the .NET Foundation as a way to bring a team together that consist of these projects spearheaded by a common goal for example we have the ASP.Net team, The Roslyn Team the .NET Core team how come we can't extend an offer and official support to the developers of relevant projects like Boo, PeachPie, IronPython, IronRuby and the many others including ETO.Forms and AvaloniaUI to become a unified foundation with the goal of making dotnetcore a defacto standard with all the love bells and jingles of a xplat framework that supports not just C# and VB.Net but all the other languages that have already been proven to run on the CLR with full development capabilities including a UI kit, Web, API, Mobile and IOT out the box. It's a shame... Just my opinion..

Hi mitchellrj,

You are among several people (myself included) that is waiting on DNS01 support. Are you willing to work on this manual plugin yourself, or did you intend this as a jump-off point for someone else? (thanks in either case)
I am not part of the letsencrypt team, but am willing to (pre-)review your code if you are looking for comments.

I have 3 comments after scanning over the code quickly:
 - Some kind of code-sharing between both (HTTP01 and DNS01) manual plugins might be a good idea. Even better if a TLSSNI01 manual mode could use that same shared code. I'm not sure if one plugin or multiple plugins would be best.
 - You are using tldextract, but this might not correspond to the actual zone configuration the user has configured. It might be better to use dns.resolver.zone_for_name, this also prevents one new dependency for the client.
 - The plugin name "dns" might be a bit ambiguous for this plugin. I am working on a RFC 2136 plugin and there are conceivably a lot more DNS plugins that could be created.

As said, if you want me to look at the code in some more detail, I am happy to do that.

This appears be related to pyca/cryptography#2503, which is solved with reaperhulk/cryptography@e702453665bcd85ef6c0ffe931db559ad8c184d8. As a workaround, you can run the following:

    $ source ~/.local/share/letsencrypt/bin/activate
    $ pip install git+git://github.com/reaperhulk/cryptography.git@e702453665bcd85ef6c0ffe931db559ad8c184d8
    $ deactivate

This should solve the problem until the next cryptography release is out.

> what's the right syntax to append an OR clause to the {% if glob[name_version] %} line that also checks the value of my product101 element?

It should be something like 
```
{% if glob[name_version] or about_object.product101.value %}
```
> is it ok to have such an element be x or null, or does it always need a value such as Y or N ?

It is okay to have 'x' or 'null' as this is a custom column and will be treated as String field.

> does the element need to be in my .ABOUT files, or can it simply be a column in my input control file?

The element needs to be in the .ABOUT files. `attrib` process get all the information from .ABOUT files.

@delcypher : Indeed, you _can_ use the ackermannization for this type of query, but you don't need `bvarray2uf`. The reason that `ackermannize_bv` didn't have the intended result is that it is limited to introducing only a very limited number of additional constraints (by default) and you had no way to guess the magic option name to change that limit (yeah, we need to clean that up...): 

```
(apply (using-params ackermannize_bv :div0_ackermann_limit 1000000))
```

And, using that information to build a sat checking tactic:

```
(check-sat-using (then (using-params ackermannize_bv :div0_ackermann_limit 1000000) simplify bit-blast sat))
```

yields unsat in 21 seconds on my machine.

>  the open bracket [ means that START is not a keyword until it encounters a closing bracket.

That *is* context sensitivity, with respect to lexical analysis.  Sometimes "start" is one thing, and sometimes it's another.  Of course we as humans can see the difference and don't get confused.  The lexer is pretty dumb.

If we made every string of letters into some kind of generic "ID" token (i.e. an "identifier"), then you'd have generic parser rules, like:

```antlr
startStopQualifier : ID StringLiteral ID StringLiteral ;
```

And then something like `hello '2018-06-14T19:16:29Z' world '2018-06-14T19:17:12Z'` would be parser-grammar-valid.  You *could* check that at a higher level, but it's nice if it can be caught earlier in the processing pipeline.  So "start" was lex'd into a special start token and referenced in the parser grammar.  It was like that when I got the grammar(s), and it made sense to me at the time.  I'm no expert on parsing, but it makes sense to me to keep things as simple as possible until there's a demonstrated need for something more complicated.

Here is my code to generate a sampling grid that does not exhibit unexpected/undesired shear artifacts.
The key idea is to apply scaling of grid coordinates from [-1,1] to pixel space *before* applying Theta (the affine transform input to the TransformerLayer).
The code below is not elegant, and any suggestions on making it simpler would be appreciated.
As to integrating this computation into special.py, I'd propose to put it in _transform_affine( ), and be sure to remove the scaling computations from _interpolate( )

=========================================
def compute_sampling_grid(theta, imgs, downsample_factor):
    """
    Deep-dive into the computations performed by
    lasagne.layers.special.TransformerLayer in particular, this function
    pulls together code from
    _meshgrid() and _transform_affine() 
    we thus  get the warped sampling grid.

     """
    floatX_t = theano.config.floatX

    num_batch, num_channels, height, width = imgs.shape
    theta = theta.reshape((2, 3))

    out_height = np.array(height // downsample_factor[0]).astype('int64')
    out_width = np.array(width // downsample_factor[1]).astype('int64')

    # symbolic computations:
    Imgs = T.tensor4()
    Theta = T.matrix()

    sym_nr = T.scalar()
    sym_nc = T.scalar()
    sym_nr_out = T.scalar()
    sym_nc_out = T.scalar()

    # Grid will be in [-1,1]x[-1,1]
    # and will have num_rows, num_cols defined by "V" in Fig 3. of the paper
    Grid = lasagne.layers.special._meshgrid(T.cast(sym_nr_out,'int64'), T.cast(sym_nc_out,'int64'))

    # We now modify the transform Theta to not only apply the desired affine xform,
    # but to also map the grid coords in the pixel space of image "U" in Fig. 3 of the paper
    Scale_ = T.eye(3)
    Scale__  = T.set_subtensor(Scale_[0,0],sym_nc/2.0)
    Scale = T.set_subtensor(Scale__[1,1],sym_nr/2.0)
    # we also adjust the original Theta so that the translation component is in the pixel space of U
    Theta_ = T.set_subtensor(Theta[0,2],(0.5+Theta[0,2])*sym_nc)
    Theta__ = T.set_subtensor(Theta_[1,2],(0.5+Theta[1,2])*sym_nr)

    # this multiplication of two 3x3 matrices with block structure has the effect of changing the
    # rotation component of Theta__ by scaling each coordinate *prior* to rotation
    Theta2 = T.dot(Theta__,Scale)

    Grid_xformed = T.dot(Theta2, Grid) # in pixel coords of U, but possibly outside image bounds
    
    sym_x = Grid_xformed[0,:].flatten() 
    sym_y = Grid_xformed[1,:].flatten()
    sym_downsample = T.vector()

 
    # clip to image bounds
    sym_x = T.clip(sym_x, 0, sym_nc-1)
    sym_y = T.clip(sym_y, 0, sym_nr-1)

    sz = sym_x.shape[0]

   sym_x = T.reshape(sym_x,(1,sz))
    sym_y = T.reshape(sym_y,(1,sz))
    Grid_xformed_pixels = T.concatenate([sym_x, sym_y], axis=0) 

    f_xform_grid = theano.function([Theta,sym_nr,sym_nc,sym_nr_out,sym_nc_out],[Theta2,Grid,Grid_xformed])

 """
 ...and so on...
 """

I agree with giving users a better warning message about which properties are missing, but I think this code should raise an Exception rather than returning `NaN`. 

When we read in the Magpie data files, we already put `NaN`s when a property is missing for a certain element. So, there should only be `KeyErrors` when the user of MagpieData passes a property that doesn't exist. I think missing (or misspelled) properties should return a error rather than a warning message.

Hi,
Yes this is intentional. In IdaDataBase.as_idadataframe it is about creating a new table containing the given PandasDataFrame. 
Table names should be unique in a schema. As a consequence, the clear existing option is false per default, just to make sure you don't drop a table unintentionally. 

However, if the names and numbers of columns and data types matches, you can use IdaDataBase.append
http://pythonhosted.org/ibmdbpy/base.html#append

Your workaround is actually the way it is meant to be. 
Do you find it non intuitive?
How would you imagine this feature? 

It is always great to have user's feedback. :)

Cheers,
Edouard

Instead of setting fields directly on the tsne object, how about calling a method on the t-sne class, e.g. (`this.tsne.setSupervision(factor, column, unlabeledClass)`). This way you are saying that supervision requires `factor`, `column` as well as `unlabeledClass`. Also no need to pass `labelCounts` if the t-sne class can derivate that internally from `labels`. Including an `encoding` field should be fine; I can support that change. But there are two significant problems with including the proposed `type` field:
 1. Requiring that all images for a given tag be of the same size and/or that each tag have a constant number of images across steps is a significant restriction of the API that we do not want to introduce. See comments on Google-internal change 161843301.
 2. Even if we accept that restriction—which I recommend that we not do—encoding the shape into the summary metadata would require that the shape be known _statically_, because the metadata is provided with the call to `tf.summary.tensor_summary`. This is definitely not acceptable: it means that `image.summary.op(tf.decode_png(input_data))` is illegal, it means that if you want to change some hyperparameters you need to recompute your graph, etc.
This is a harmless warning that comes from our TensorFlow dependency, which can be ignored, or you can build TensorFlow from source, so CPU computations go faster for your hardware.

```C++
template <typename Type, typename Alloc> struct type_caster<std::vector<Type, Alloc>>
 : list_caster<std::vector<Type, Alloc>, Type> { };
```

so you should be able to add your own definition like the above, but change the `std::vector<Type, Alloc>` to your own class.

(I should point out in passing that inheriting from stl containers is generally frowned upon, most notably because they don't have virtual destructors).

I don't see how a runtime check is possible practically (at least without a ridiculous amount of overhead)--we have a structure:
```
Bbase     std::enable_s_f_t<B>
     \   /
       B
```
with no way to determine, from a `Bbase &`, that the instance is actually *some* `enable_shared_from_this<T>` for some `T`.  But even if we could (for example, by checking all of `Bbase`'s known descendant classes), we'd still be talking about a huge run-time penalty incurred on every cast for a feature that is only very rarely used.

The current approach works by deducing `T`, but such deduction only works when we are working with `B`, not when we work from `Bbase`.

The only way I could practically see to do this would be to have some `internals` data that stores a list of subclass-via-`shared_from_this` lambdas that get assigned when a registered subclass for a type inheriting from `shared_from_this` is added.  But even that would be fairly heavy, and probably not all that easy to implement.

> One more question: is there ever a situation when your patch causes a chaining behavior? I thought I remembered something like that when looking at your testcases, though perhaps I misunderstood.

No, chaining won't happen--the code only looks at implicit conversions that have been registered with the argument type as the output type.  So `py::i_c<A,B>(); py::i_c<B,C>()` won't consider the A->B conversion when looking for a way to convert the arguments to an argument of type C.  If would have to be a bit more complicated to allow that to also detect and avoid chaining loops, but it would also be implementing a C++-level implicit conversion beyond what C++ actually implements.  There's also a second issue with chaining in that we don't allow implicit conversion between two unregistered types (e.g. A->B, B->C, with both B and C unregistered).

So I suppose that, in order to use this mechanism for multiple inheritance, it would involve checking in implicitly_convertible<A,B> whether std::is_base_of<B, A> and, if so, storing a function that simply does a static_cast<A*>(b) (rather than constructing a `new A` from the B).  It wouldn't need the constructor or destructor, but we could still store the base class pointer in implicit_instances, with a flag to say "don't delete this" during `function_call_internals` destruction.
The docs suggests to read the return value policies carefully and mentions that auto is not always the right policy. PRs which improve the docs are welcomed, if you have concrete suggestions.

There already is a table of exception mapping from C++ to Python
https://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/stable/advanced/exceptions.html

All other Python exceptions not in that table are raised on the C++ as `std::exception`. So `ImportError` on failing to load a module will be raised as `std::exception` unless you register a specific handler. A C++ exception can be trapped in Python or C++, and a Python exception can be trapped in Python or C++.

I agree that this page should have some commentary on exceptions and will add that when I have some time.

I don't think that either a requirements.txt nor a Pipfile are a good approach for dealing with library dependencies. I do, however, think that a Pipfile.lock (and thus a Pipfile) is a really good approach for dealing with development dependencies and pinning dependencies for development. I find Pipfile and pipenv to be superior to just using pip and requirements.txt files.

What this means practically is that I want all the generic dependencies to be kept in the `setup.py` file, but I'd also be happy to see a Pipfile added to make for simpler development. The only non-dev dependency the Pipfile would have is on this package, but it could have lots of dev dependencies, and I think the Pipfile.lock could be helpful for development. We should make sure that the dependency on this library is using an editable install, and then pinning just dev dependencies shouldn't be an issue.

@ericl That approach sounds good. The only problem I can foresee is we won't cancel requests when we no longer need them (only terminating the node once it starts); this problem seems minor, however.

An alternative that avoids this problem (and does not require threading) would be to have create_node make a [RequestSpotInstance](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/APIReference/API_RequestSpotInstances.html) call, which should always return immediately. nodes() would be changed to check both the running instances and unfulfilled spot instance requests. (There's potentially a race here, but it seems like it would happen very rarely, and would not have major consequences.) {internal,external}_ip would return None while the instance request is pending.

The only change I can see this requiring to the autoscaler would having _update preferentially terminate nodes without a recorded last_used time. However, I'm not familiar with this part of the code base so I may have overlooked some other implications.

Any thoughts on pros/cons of this compared to alternative approaches? I'll try and carve out some time later this week to implement whatever we decide on.

This is a great point. The assumption is that all the data from the task doesn't fit in the object store, right? In situations where we have plenty of memory and reconstruction is only being triggered due to node failures, this example should work, right?

In the case where things don't fit in memory, it'll be tough to handle this example correctly. Spilling over to disk would extend the range of situations that get handled appropriately, but we could come up with workloads where that wouldn't save us.

Btw, I think the same problem is present even without the initial `ray.put`. if you just did `arg = array`, then the first time you called `single_dependency.remote(arg)`, `ray.put` would be called under the hood on the argument (this happens for basically all large or complicated arguments).

kube_config = os.getenv('KUBE_CONFIG')
